report: The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, evolving into tonic stiffening of the arms with clenched fists. This is followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and head, progressing to asynchronous shaking. Fencer posturing occurs with left arm flexion and right arm extension during the episode.
The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking. This evolves into unilateral right arm flexion and tonic stiffening of the right side, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, progressing to tonic stiffening involving the trunk and limbs. This is followed by bilateral clonic jerks affecting the face, arms, and legs, with asynchronous shaking; rhythmic jerking also prominently involves the head and arms, accompanied by clenched fists.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.
Blank stare, then rhythmic jerks of the right arm and leg; later bilateral tonic–clonic movements with clenched fists and stiffened limbs.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic jerks.
Blank stare, then bilateral tonic stiffening with eyes rolling back; progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face, arms, and legs.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic jerks.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion and head version to the right. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking, progressing to a fencer-like posturing with left arm flexion and right arm extension.

The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset of each episode, occasionally accompanied by subtle head version (typically rightward), lip smacking, or right arm movement (flexion or extension). This initial phase rapidly progresses to tonic stiffening involving the trunk, upper body, and limbs. Clonic jerks begin unilaterally—often in the right arm and leg—and spread to involve both sides, resulting in bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking. In some episodes, fencer posturing is observed, characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension. All episodes conclude with a postictal state marked by unresponsiveness and the patient lying still.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which progresses to tonic stiffening involving the trunk, limbs, bilateral arm extension, and leg flexion. This is followed by bilateral clonic jerks affecting the arms and legs. The movements are predominantly synchronous, though some asynchrony in shaking is noted. The episode persists with rhythmic jerking until its conclusion, during which the patient becomes unresponsive and remains so until lying still at the end of the event.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, which progresses to rightward head version and right arm extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with synchronous and generalized jerking of the arms and legs, though some limb jerking is asynchronous. Tonic stiffening is present, accompanied by facial grimacing, and the patient becomes unresponsive during the episode.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, progressing to tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and head retraction, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face, arms, and legs. The movements are initially synchronous and sustained, becoming asynchronous during the clonic phase, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and leg, with the left arm remaining relatively still. The jerks progress to involve the entire right side of the body, while the left side shows minimal movement. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.
The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by lip smacking and right head version. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of the limbs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and shoulder, with no clear tonic phase. The movements remain unilateral and do not spread to the contralateral side or involve the legs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and head, with the left arm remaining relatively still. The jerking progresses to involve the entire right side of the body, including the face and trunk, while the left side shows minimal movement. The episode ends with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the head and upper limbs. The movements are bilateral and asynchronous, with no clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, initially accompanied by subtle lip smacking and rightward head version in some episodes. This behavioral arrest may be followed by rhythmic clonic jerks that begin focally—either in the face and upper limbs or in the right arm and leg—and subsequently spread to involve bilateral limb and facial musculature, evolving into generalized bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The jerking is asynchronous and involves the entire body, including side-to-side head motion. No clear unilateral predominance is observed during the generalized phase. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the event and returns to a relaxed state with closed eyes after cessation of motor activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and head, with the left arm remaining relatively still. The jerks progress to involve the entire right side of the body, including the face and trunk, while the left side shows minimal movement. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by tonic stiffening of the upper body and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face, upper limbs, and right arm and leg. Automatisms include lip smacking and hand clenching, with right arm flexion observed during the episode. The clinical course includes evolution into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking, spreading to involve the entire body. One episode features a fencer posture (right arm and leg extension with contralateral arm flexion), which progresses to generalized bilateral tonic–clonic activity. In all cases, the patient is unresponsive at the end, with no clear unilateral predominance in the bilateral, asynchronous motor manifestations.
The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face, head, and upper limbs. The movements are bilateral and asynchronous, with no clear unilateral predominance.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms, legs, head, and upper body. The movements are bilateral and asynchronous, with no clear unilateral predominance or pattern. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, which evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking lasting several seconds. The episode resolves into postictal confusion. At other times, the patient is lying supine with eyes closed, appears unresponsive, and shows no visible signs of tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms, or asymmetric posturing, remaining motionless throughout.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with tonic stiffening, bilateral arm extension, clenched fists, and asynchronous or rhythmic jerking involving the head, arms, and legs.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, progressing to tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and clenched fists. This evolves into rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, with asynchronous shaking. The episode includes rightward head version and right arm extension, followed by fencer posturing (left flexion with right extension). The event concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive and limp.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, which evolves into unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right in some episodes. This is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements, characterized by asynchronous jerking involving the limbs and trunk in certain instances, while other episodes show more rhythmic jerking of the limbs and head. One episode includes fencer posturing with left arm flexion and right arm extension following the tonic–clonic phase. The patient becomes unresponsive at the end of the episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking, which evolves into tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs with bilateral arm extension and head retraction. This is followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, characterized by asynchronous shaking. The episode concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, progressing to tonic stiffening involving the neck, upper limbs, and bilateral arm extension with clenched fists. This is followed by generalized tonic–clonic movements affecting the limbs and trunk, including bilateral clonic jerks of the arms and legs. During the clonic phase, fencer-like posturing is observed, characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with no discernible automatisms or clonic movements initially, followed by progression to bilateral hand clenching with facial grimacing, eyes closed, and head slightly bowed. This is accompanied by sustained bilateral hand clenching and facial grimacing, evolving into rhythmic jerking of the arms and legs. In one instance, right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture) is observed prior to the onset of rhythmic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no visible tonic stiffening or unilateral arm movements outside of the fencer presentation, and no other seizure activity is observed.
Blank stare, followed by right arm flexion with left extension (fencer), then rhythmic jerks; unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by tonic stiffening of the entire body, progressing to bilateral clonic jerks involving the face, arms, legs, and trunk, with the head turning to the right. The movements are synchronous and rhythmic, continuing for several seconds before gradually subsiding.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, which progresses to rightward head version and right arm flexion in one observed episode. This is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by tonic stiffening of the upper body and asynchronous clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The seizure evolves into generalized convulsions with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso. After the convulsive phase, the patient becomes unresponsive with closed eyes and relaxed muscles.
Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, which is followed by motor manifestations that vary across episodes. In one instance, this evolves into unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In another episode, rhythmic clonic jerks involve the arms and legs bilaterally in an asynchronous pattern, without clear unilateral predominance. A third episode features rhythmic clonic jerks initially localized to the right arm and leg, progressing to the entire right side of the body, while the left side shows minimal involvement. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive. The events resolve with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.
Blank stare, followed by either right arm flexion with left extension (fencer) or bilateral tonic–clonic movements, then rhythmic jerks. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, followed by evolution into tonic stiffening characterized by bilateral arm extension and head retraction. Right arm flexion with left arm extension in a fencer-like posture is observed, then progresses to asynchronous, rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, which persist until the end of the recording. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the later phases of the event.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, then progression to bilateral tonic–clonic movements involving arms flailing and legs stiffening. Patient becomes unresponsive with mouth open and eyes rolled back.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, rapidly progressing to tonic stiffening of the entire body with right arm flexion and left extension (fencer posture), followed by bilateral, asynchronous clonic jerks. The episode concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by motor manifestations that include bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, evolving into rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and upper extremities. Movements are typically generalized and synchronous, though some episodes show asynchronous shaking. Several events begin with right arm flexion and contralateral (left) arm extension in a "fencer" posture, which then progresses to rhythmic jerking. Additional features observed include lip smacking, right head version, and right hand clenching in individual episodes. All events evolve into bilateral tonic–clonic activity, and the patient remains unresponsive at the conclusion of each episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture) and/or rightward head version, which evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking of the limbs. Rhythmic jerks occur during the event, and the patient becomes unresponsive at the end. In one observed episode, there is right arm extension instead of flexion following the head version, before progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. In another instance, no clear early signs of seizure are visible; the patient remains unresponsive with eyes closed throughout, showing no automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or convulsive movements.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, progressing to tonic stiffening of the limbs with bilateral arm extension and head retraction. This is followed by synchronous, rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, which persist throughout the episode. During the event, the patient exhibits fencer posturing characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension.

Blank stare followed by lip smacking, progressing to tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs with bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. Fencer posturing observed in two variations: right arm flexion with left extension and left arm flexion with right extension. Patient becomes unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare, accompanied in one instance by lip smacking or subtle head version to the right. This progresses to tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, which are at times asynchronous. In one episode, this evolves into fencer posturing with left arm flexion and right arm extension. The events conclude with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.
The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and leg. The jerks spread to involve the left arm and leg, resulting in bilateral asynchronous shaking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerks involve the face, neck, and arms, with no clear unilateral pattern initially evident.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle right arm flexion, followed by left-sided extension resembling a fencing posture. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of all limbs and rhythmic head movements. The patient is unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version or lip smacking, which evolves into tonic stiffening of the upper limbs, often with bilateral arm extension and head retraction, followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the face, arms, and legs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, initially subtle in some episodes, accompanied by lip smacking and hand movements or clenching (automatisms) in certain instances. This is followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Tonic stiffening involves the trunk and upper body, sometimes presenting with fencer posturing characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension. Clonic jerking of the limbs and trunk is typically asynchronous, though episodes with synchronous and rhythmic jerking have also been observed. The patient becomes unresponsive during these events, which may progress from focal onset to generalized bilateral motor activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements involving the arms, legs, and head, with both tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks. The movements are predominantly synchronous, persist throughout the observation, and show no clear unilateral predominance, though some asynchronous shaking is noted.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, progressing to tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are synchronous and rhythmic, with the patient unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare, followed by right arm flexion with left extension (fencer), then tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, progressing to bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which progresses to unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right. This is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements involving the arms and legs, with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso. The movements are synchronous and involve the entire body, though there is an initial unilateral predominance on the right side. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking or subtle rightward head version. This is followed by focal motor activity involving the right side of the body, including rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and leg, right arm extension, and occasionally a fencer posture (right arm extended, contralateral arm flexed). The seizure activity progresses from unilateral involvement—typically starting on the right side—to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of all limbs. In some episodes, asynchronous shaking occurs during generalization. Bilateral stiffening (tonic phase) may precede or accompany the clonic activity. The left arm is initially less involved or remains still before secondary generalization. One atypical observation includes the patient maintaining partial awareness, such as holding a phone and rubbing their eye with the left hand during the blank stare. Most episodes conclude with unresponsiveness and a period of immobility post-ictally.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, which progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by tonic stiffening with arm extension and head retraction, followed by clonic jerks. The seizure activity spreads to involve the entire body with rhythmic jerking, and the patient becomes unresponsive at the end.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient lies supine with eyes closed and mouth slightly open, head and neck extended backward, arms at her sides, and remains unresponsive throughout the observation. She exhibits a blank stare followed by tonic stiffening of the entire body, which evolves into bilateral clonic jerks. The movements are rhythmic, synchronous, and involve the limbs and trunk with no clear unilateral predominance, though asynchronous shaking of the limbs is also observed in some phases.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by one of the following patterns: (1) rhythmic clonic jerks of the face and upper limbs progressing to involve the entire body with bilateral synchronous jerking and loss of responsiveness; (2) rhythmic clonic jerks involving the head and upper limbs that are bilateral and asynchronous, without clear unilateral predominance or focal onset; or (3) rightward head version with either right arm extension or right arm flexion, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by asynchronous shaking of the limbs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by a tonic phase characterized by generalized stiffening of the upper body and limbs, clenched jaw, and clenched fists. This transitions into bilateral clonic jerks involving the face, arms, legs, and trunk, with synchronous, rhythmic, and bilateral movements that show no clear unilateral predominance. In one instance, right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture) is observed prior to the onset of rhythmic jerking. The episode ends with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still, remaining unresponsive throughout the post-ictal period.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by upward eye deviation, followed by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs. This progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by synchronous, rhythmic jerking of the arms, legs, and trunk. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode, which culminates in a postictal state marked by unresponsiveness and limpness. No clear early signs of seizure are observed, and in some instances, the patient remains motionless with eyes closed, showing no automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or lateralizing features.

The patient lies supine with eyes closed and exhibits a blank stare followed by tonic stiffening of the entire body. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements involving the arms, legs, and torso, characterized by rhythmic jerking and stiffening. The movements are asynchronous and persist throughout the episode, which concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with bilateral tonic stiffening, progressing to rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are synchronous and generalized, with no clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a tonic phase with stiffening of the body, followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the limbs and trunk. The movements are rhythmic and asynchronous, beginning in the right arm and progressing to the right leg, then spreading to the left arm and leg. During the episode, the patient lies on their side with arms flexed, the head turns to the right, and the arms alternately flex and extend. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable motor manifestations. In some instances, this progresses to right arm flexion with left arm extension resembling a fencing posture, while in others, there is tonic stiffening involving the neck, upper limbs, or entire body, sometimes with head and neck extension. This tonic phase evolves into bilateral clonic jerks characterized by rhythmic flexion and extension of the arms and legs, involving the trunk in some cases. The clonic movements are generally synchronous without clear unilateral predominance, though head and eye deviation to the right is observed in one instance. No automatisms or focal clonic onset are noted in certain segments. Throughout all events, the patient remains unresponsive, with no observable seizure activity in one segment.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by various initial manifestations including rightward head version with right arm flexion, tonic stiffening of the upper body, head retraction, or upward eye deviation. These evolve into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking or jerking. In some episodes, the seizure progresses to fencer posturing (left flexion with right extension), automatisms, and rhythmic jerks. The patient becomes unresponsive during or after the motor activity and remains motionless with eyes closed, consistent with postictal unresponsiveness. In certain observations, no clear early signs or evolution of seizure activity are visible, with the patient remaining motionless and eyes closed throughout.
No clear seizure activity is observed. The patient remains still with eyes closed throughout the recording. There are no signs of automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or asymmetric posturing.

The patient lies supine with eyes closed and mouth slightly open, exhibiting a blank stare followed by tonic stiffening of the body and bilateral clonic jerks that are synchronous and involve the entire body without clear unilateral predominance. There is an episode of right arm flexion with left extension resembling a fencer posture, followed by rhythmic jerks. The patient remains motionless throughout the recording, shows no automatisms or asymmetric posturing, and is unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by tonic stiffening of the entire body. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of the limbs and trunk. The episode ends with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, which evolves into bilateral, synchronous, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are generalized with no clear unilateral predominance or pattern, although one episode began with subtle rightward head version. The patient remains unresponsive throughout each event.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, followed by initial right arm flexion with head version to the right. This progresses to tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and head retraction, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking involving the arms and legs, clenched fists, and left arm extension. The episode concludes with unresponsiveness.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with subsequent tonic stiffening of the neck and upper body; evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic activity characterized by asynchronous clonic jerks of the limbs and head.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks involving the head, upper limbs, and torso. The movements are bilateral and asynchronous, without clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare, followed by bilateral rhythmic jerking of arms and legs that spreads to the entire body, with tonic stiffening and clonic movements. Right arm flexion with left extension (fencer position) observed during onset. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.
The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face, arms, and legs. The movements are synchronous and generalized, with no clear unilateral predominance.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, progressing to a tonic phase characterized by generalized stiffening and clenched fists. Tonic stiffening initially involves the left arm and evolves into bilateral involvement, including fencer posturing (left arm flexion with right arm extension) in some instances. This is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with synchronous, rhythmic clonic jerks affecting the limbs and trunk. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by upward gaze and subtle automatisms such as lip smacking and hand clenching. This initial phase may progress to tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, with bilateral upper limb flexion and clenched fists. A fencer-like posture may develop, characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension. The episode evolves into bilateral clonic jerks, which can begin in the right arm and leg before spreading to involve the left side, ultimately progressing to generalized tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic shaking of the arms, legs, and head being thrown back. The eyes remain closed throughout, and the patient is unresponsive to verbal stimuli, with loss of consciousness during the event.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity with right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), or bilateral tonic stiffening with clenched fists; both patterns progress to rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and limbs. Unresponsive at the end.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic jerks.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with subtle lip smacking, progressing to rightward head version and right arm extension, along with right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posturing), followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and upper limbs. These movements are bilateral and asynchronous, without clear unilateral predominance, and evolve into tonic stiffening of the upper body followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The episode concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right head version and right arm involvement, which manifests as either extension or flexion. In one instance, right arm extension is observed, while in another, right arm flexion occurs alongside left arm extension in a fencer-like posture. Lip smacking is noted during the blank stare phase. These initial signs progress to rhythmic jerking and evolve into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous limb jerking. The patient becomes unresponsive at the conclusion of the episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and upper limbs, initially observed in the right arm and leg before spreading to become bilateral and asynchronous. The movements are bilateral without clear unilateral predominance, and the patient becomes unresponsive at the end of the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by lip smacking, which evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by synchronous, rhythmic jerking of the arms, legs, and torso. The episode includes initial subtle oral automatisms, progresses to asymmetric motor activity with right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posturing), and culminates in generalized tonic–clonic activity involving the entire body. The event concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient presents with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, followed by evolution into rightward head version with right arm extension, then unilateral right arm flexion and rhythmic jerking. The motor activity subsequently spreads to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In one instance, the episode begins with a blank stare and bilateral, asynchronous clonic jerks of the head and upper limbs without clear unilateral predominance, also progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. All episodes conclude with the patient lying still, eyes closed, and a relaxed facial expression.

The patient exhibits a blank stare accompanied by rightward head version and right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture), which progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking of the arms and legs. The episode concludes with a post-ictal state marked by closed eyes and unresponsiveness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer sign), head version to the right, and deviation of the eyes to the right. This progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with synchronous jerking involving the entire body, although some asynchronous shaking is also observed. The episode includes lip smacking and tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, culminating in unresponsiveness.
No clear early signs or evolution of seizure activity are visible. The patient remains motionless with eyes closed throughout the recording.

Initial blank stare with lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer-like posturing), progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asymmetric jerking. The episode evolves into generalized convulsions with loss of responsiveness, including a phase of left arm extension and right arm flexion, then generalized tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle head version and tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs. This evolves into bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, with asynchronous shaking in some episodes and rhythmic shaking in others. The movements persist throughout the event, which may include rapid progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive during the episode and becomes still at its conclusion.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version or lip smacking, which rapidly evolves into tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, followed by bilateral, asynchronous clonic jerks. The episode concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive and limp.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version or lip smacking, followed by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs. This evolves into bilateral clonic jerks, progressing to tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking involving the arms and legs; in some phases, the jerking is synchronous and rhythmic. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which evolves into unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, rapidly evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs followed by asynchronous clonic jerking of the limbs and trunk. The episode concludes with post-ictal unresponsiveness.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, accompanied by lip smacking. This progresses to unilateral right arm flexion and continued head version to the right, evolving into bilateral tonic stiffening with arm extension. Subsequently, asynchronous clonic jerks develop, involving the limbs and trunk and becoming more generalized and violent, with the patient arching their back and thrashing against the bed.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and right leg extension in a fencing posture, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and head. The patient is unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion with left extension in a fencer's posture, and bilateral arm extension with head retraction indicative of a tonic phase. This progresses into a clonic phase characterized by rhythmic jerking of the limbs. The seizure concludes with a postictal state marked by generalized flaccidity and unresponsiveness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking of the limbs, progressing to fencer posturing with left arm flexion and right arm extension.

The patient begins with a blank stare accompanied by subtle head version and lip smacking. This progresses to unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by tonic stiffening with arm extension and leg flexion, then asynchronous clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. Automatisms and fencer-like posturing are observed during the event.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer's posture), which evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Rhythmic jerks occur during the episode, and the patient is unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, which progresses to rightward head version and right arm extension. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and leg flexion, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the limbs and trunk. The movements are asynchronous and persist throughout the episode.

Initial right arm flexion and head version to the right, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asymmetric jerking. The patient exhibits fencer-like posturing with left arm extension and right arm flexion, progressing to generalized convulsions. Automatisms include lip smacking and rhythmic leg movements, with the patient becoming unresponsive during the event. Tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs frequently follows the initial focal phase, progressing to bilateral clonic jerks that are typically asynchronous and involve the arms, legs, face, and head. Generalization occurs in all episodes, manifesting as bilateral tonic–clonic movements with clenched fists, stiffened legs, and rhythmic, asynchronous shaking.
